extra.message:
Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled Globally

Jinja2 is configured with `autoescape=False`, which disables HTML escaping for all template variables globally. This means any user-controlled data rendered in templates will not be escaped, enabling stored or reflected XSS attacks across the entire application.

Impact: Attackers can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, and other XSS-based attacks.

Fix: Set `autoescape=True` (or use `select_autoescape(['html', 'jinja2'])`) in the Jinja2 setup. Explicitly use `| safe` only for trusted, sanitized content.

SQL Injection via String Formatting in Student.create()

The `Student.create()` method constructs the SQL query using Python's `%` string formatting operator directly with user-supplied `name` input: `"VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}`. This is NOT parameterized query execution — the value is interpolated into the SQL string before being passed to `cur.execute()`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by supplying a crafted name such as `'); DROP TABLE students; --` or use UNION-based injection to exfiltrate data.

Impact: Full SQL injection — attacker can execute arbitrary SQL statements, exfiltrate all data (including user credentials, PII), modify or delete records, and potentially escalate to OS-level access via PostgreSQL functions.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `q = 'INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)'` and `await cur.execute(q, (name,))`. Never use Python string formatting to interpolate user data into SQL strings.

XSS via Unescaped review_text in course.jinja2

The `review.review_text` field is rendered directly in the template without escaping. Since autoescape is globally disabled and this is user-supplied content stored in the database, this is a stored XSS vulnerability.

Impact: Attackers can store malicious JavaScript in course reviews that executes in the browsers of all users who view the course page, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and other attacks.

Fix: Use `{{ review.review_text | e }}` to HTML-escape the output, or enable autoescape globally.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in courses.jinja2

The `course.title` is rendered without escaping in the courses list. With autoescape disabled, stored XSS payloads in course titles will execute.

Impact: Stored XSS affecting all users who view the courses listing page.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable autoescape globally.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in student.jinja2

The `course.title` is rendered without escaping in the student detail page.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, executing for any user viewing a student's results page.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable autoescape globally.

XSS via Unescaped student.name in students.jinja2

The student `name` field is rendered without escaping in the students list. With autoescape disabled, stored XSS payloads in student names will execute.

Impact: Stored XSS affecting all users who view the students listing page.

Fix: Use `{{ name | e }}` or enable autoescape globally.

CSRF Middleware Commented Out

The `csrf_middleware` is commented out in the application middleware stack. While CSRF tokens are generated and included in forms, they are never validated server-side because the middleware that performs validation is disabled. All state-changing POST endpoints (login, logout, create student, create course, evaluate, review) are unprotected against CSRF attacks.

Impact: Attackers can craft malicious pages that cause authenticated users to perform unintended actions such as creating students/courses, submitting evaluations, or logging out.

Fix: Remove the comment to re-enable `csrf_middleware` in the middleware stack.

Weak Password Hashing with MD5

The `check_password()` method uses MD5 to hash passwords: `md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()`. MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash function that is extremely fast to compute, making it trivial to crack via brute force or rainbow table attacks. There is no evidence of salting, making the hashes even more vulnerable.

Impact: If the database is compromised (e.g., via the SQL injection vulnerability), all user passwords can be rapidly cracked using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force, leading to full account takeover and credential stuffing attacks on other services.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a proper password hashing algorithm such as `bcrypt`, `argon2`, or `scrypt` via the `passlib` library. These algorithms are slow by design and include built-in salting.

Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, which means the session cookie is accessible to JavaScript. This makes session tokens vulnerable to theft via XSS attacks.

Impact: If an XSS vulnerability is exploited (which is likely given autoescape=False), attackers can steal session cookies via JavaScript, enabling full session hijacking.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` on the RedisStorage to prevent JavaScript access to session cookies.

XSS via Unescaped auth_user name fields in base.jinja2

The authenticated user's `first_name`, `middle_name`, and `last_name` are rendered without escaping in the navigation bar. With autoescape disabled, if these fields contain HTML/JS, XSS will execute for the authenticated user.

Impact: Stored XSS via user name fields, executing for the authenticated user on every page load.

Fix: Use `{{ auth_user.first_name | e }}` etc., or enable autoescape globally.

Information Disclosure via error.__dict__ in 50x.jinja2

The 500 error template renders `error.__dict__` directly, exposing all internal attributes of the exception object to users. This can reveal stack traces, internal paths, configuration details, and other sensitive information.

Impact: Attackers can obtain detailed internal application information from server errors, including file paths, configuration, and internal state that aids further exploitation.

Fix: Remove `{{ error.__dict__ }}` from the error template. Log the full error server-side and show only a generic error message to users.

XSS via Unescaped evaluate.jinja2 fieldname and errors

The `fieldname` and `errors` variables from validation errors are rendered without escaping. While these come from the schema validator, if any field names or error messages contain user-controlled content, XSS is possible.

Impact: Potential XSS if field names or error messages contain user-controlled data.

Fix: Use `{{ fieldname | e }}` and `{{ errors | e }}` or enable autoescape globally.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in review.jinja2

The `course.title` is rendered without escaping in the review form card title.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, executing for users who visit the review submission page.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable autoescape globally.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint (POST /)

The login handler (POST to `/`) has no rate limiting or brute-force protection. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force credentials.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force or credential stuffing attacks against the login endpoint without any throttling.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., limit by IP address, add account lockout after N failed attempts, or use CAPTCHA).

Missing Session Regeneration After Login (Session Fixation)

After successful authentication, the session ID is not regenerated. The existing session is reused with the new `user_id` set. This allows session fixation attacks where an attacker pre-sets a known session ID and then waits for the victim to authenticate.

Impact: An attacker who can set a victim's session cookie (e.g., via network interception or XSS) can fix the session ID before login and then use that same session ID to access the authenticated session after the victim logs in.

Fix: Regenerate the session ID after successful authentication by creating a new session and invalidating the old one.

Missing Authentication on Students POST Endpoint

The `students` view handler accepts POST requests to create new students without any authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can create student records.

Impact: Unauthenticated attackers can create arbitrary student records, polluting the database with PII data.

Fix: Apply the `@authorize()` decorator or add an explicit authentication check before processing POST requests in the students handler.

Missing Authorization Check on Student Creation (POST /students/)

Even if authentication were enforced, the `students` handler does not check whether the authenticated user has admin privileges before creating a student. Any authenticated user (not just admins) could create students.

Impact: Any authenticated user can create student records, bypassing the intended admin-only restriction implied by the template's `{% if auth_user %}` gate.

Fix: Use `@authorize(ensure_admin=True)` or add an explicit admin check before processing the POST request.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production

The aiohttp Application is initialized with `debug=True`. This enables verbose error reporting and may expose internal application details, stack traces, and configuration information to users.

Impact: Detailed error messages and stack traces may be exposed to attackers, revealing internal application structure, file paths, and logic that can aid further exploitation.

Fix: Set `debug=False` in production, or derive the debug flag from a configuration value.

Missing Redis Authentication Password in Configuration Schema

The Redis configuration schema defines only `host`, `port`, and `db` fields — there is no `password` field. The Redis connection in `services/redis.py` also does not pass any authentication credentials. Redis instances without authentication are accessible to anyone who can reach the network port, allowing unauthorized read/write access to session data.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis port can read, modify, or delete session data (including authentication sessions), potentially hijacking authenticated user sessions or escalating privileges.

Fix: Add an optional `password` field to the Redis config schema and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()` via the `password` parameter. In production, always configure Redis with a strong password and bind it to localhost or a private network.

Database Connection String Without SSL/TLS

The database connection DSN is constructed without any SSL/TLS parameters (`sslmode`, `sslcert`, etc.). All communication between the application and PostgreSQL database occurs in plaintext, exposing credentials and PII data to network interception.

Impact: Network-level attackers (man-in-the-middle) can intercept database credentials and all query results including PII (student names, grades, user data) transmitted between the application and database.

Fix: Add `sslmode=require` (or `sslmode=verify-full` for production) to the DSN string and configure the PostgreSQL server with SSL certificates.

Redis Connection Without Authentication

The Redis connection pool is created without any authentication credentials. The `aioredis.create_pool()` call only passes `host`, `port`, and `db` — no `password` parameter. This means the application will connect to Redis without authentication, which is insecure if Redis is exposed beyond localhost.

Impact: Unauthenticated access to Redis allows session hijacking, session forgery, and potential privilege escalation by manipulating session data stored in Redis.

Fix: Add a `password` configuration option and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()`: `password=conf.get('password')`.

auth_user_processor Passes Full User Object to Template Context

The `auth_user_processor` context processor passes the full `User` model object to every template. If the User model contains sensitive fields such as password hashes, tokens, or other internal data, these are exposed in the template context and could be accidentally rendered.

Impact: Sensitive user fields (password hash, internal IDs, tokens) are available in every template context, increasing the risk of accidental exposure.

Fix: Pass only the necessary safe fields to the template context rather than the full User model object.

Missing Rate Limiting on Course Creation (POST /courses/)

The POST handler for `/courses/` has no rate limiting. An attacker can flood the endpoint with requests to create unlimited course records.

Impact: An attacker can exhaust database resources or create massive amounts of junk data.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the `/courses/` POST endpoint.

Incorrect SQL Query Construction in Course.get_many() — Potential Injection via Malformed Parameterization

The `get_many()` method in `Course` builds a query with `LIMIT + %(limit)s` and `OFFSET + %(offset)s` (note the `+` operator in the SQL string), and then calls `cur.execute(q, **params)` — passing `params` as keyword arguments rather than as a positional dict argument. The `**params` unpacking is incorrect for `aiopg`'s `execute()` method which expects `(query, parameters)` where parameters is a dict or tuple. This may cause runtime errors or unexpected behavior. While `limit` and `offset` are typed as `Optional[int]`, if type coercion is bypassed, the `+` in the SQL could interact unexpectedly.

Impact: Incorrect parameterization could lead to query failures or, in edge cases where type validation is bypassed, unexpected SQL behavior. The `+` operator in `LIMIT + %(limit)s` is unusual SQL syntax.

Fix: Fix the `execute()` call to pass `params` as a positional argument: `await cur.execute(q, params)`. Also remove the `+` from `LIMIT + %(limit)s` and `OFFSET + %(offset)s` as it is not standard SQL syntax.

Missing Input Validation on Student Name Length

The student name field has no server-side length validation. An attacker can submit arbitrarily long strings that may cause database errors or resource exhaustion.

Impact: Potential database errors or resource exhaustion from excessively long input.

Fix: Add server-side validation to limit the length and format of student names.
